steps.title: Identify the predicted defensive shift
steps.content: The conclusion predicts that whitefly chemicals cause plants to preferentially defend against microbial infection by shifting toward salicylic acid production and away from jasmonic acid production when subsequently infested with whiteflies — that is, less jasmonic acid and more salicylic acid compared to control plants.

steps.title: Match the data to the predicted pattern
steps.content:
Plants exposed to whitefly-infested air produce approximately 100 nanograms of jasmonic acid (less than the 140 nanogram control level) and approximately 175 nanograms of salicylic acid (more than the 120 nanogram control level), demonstrating the predicted defensive shift. Choice A directly describes this pattern and contrasts it with plants exposed to whitefly-free air, which show more jasmonic acid and thus preserved herbivory defense — establishing that the shift occurs specifically in response to whitefly signals. Choices B, C, and D mischaracterize the acid levels, falsely claim exclusive salicylic production, or describe the data without emphasizing the critical shift in defensive priorities.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
